title: Communicating with Data
summary: How to turn a chart into a message — stripping clutter, using color and annotation as tools, and building the habits that make data communication consistently clear.

chapters.title: Story Before Chart
chapters.summary: The chart is the last thing you build. Before touching a visualization tool, you need to know what question you're answering, for whom, and what you want them to do with the answer.

chapters.title: Stripping the Chart
chapters.summary: Most charts have too much on them. Removing the elements that don't help the reader understand the data is the fastest way to make a chart more communicative.

chapters.title: Color as a Communication Tool
chapters.summary: Color should do one thing: direct the reader's attention. The single-highlight technique — everything gray except the one element that carries the message — is consistently more effective than multi-color schemes.

chapters.title: Annotation as Message
chapters.summary: The title is the most important element on the chart — not the axes, not the legend. An interpretive title combined with targeted annotation eliminates ambiguity about what the reader should take away.

chapters.title: Chart Sequencing in Presentations
chapters.summary: A single chart communicates one point. A sequence of charts builds an argument. Knowing how to order charts, when to introduce complexity, and how to link each chart to the next is what separates a data presentation from a data dump.

chapters.title: Dashboard Patterns
chapters.summary: Dashboards are not collections of charts. They're answers to recurring questions — and the difference between a dashboard that gets used and one that doesn't is whether it was designed around the question or around the available data.

chapters.title: Live Presentation vs Sent Document
chapters.summary: A chart that works beautifully in a slide deck falls apart when emailed — and vice versa. The design decisions that serve live presentation actively hurt async comprehension.

chapters.title: Mobile-First and Real-Time Chart Design
chapters.summary: A chart designed for a 1440px desktop becomes illegible at 375px mobile — and a real-time dashboard that refreshes every second is solving a different problem than a weekly business review chart.

chapters.title: Common Pitfalls and Misleading Charts
chapters.summary: Most misleading charts aren't deliberately deceptive — they're the result of default settings, lazy choices, and unexamined conventions. Knowing the patterns makes them easy to spot and correct.

chapters.title: Building the Habit
chapters.summary: Good data communication is a craft, not a talent. The patterns that turn deliberate practice into fluency — the critique habit, the personal chart library, and the team norms that make everyone's charts better over time.
